title: The Great Un-monolithing
shortDesc: One giant app became many small, well-behaved services. Readers never noticed. That was the point.
body:
The old system was one enormous application that did everything and feared nothing, least of all my weekends. We took it apart piece by piece: a message queue in the middle, services around it, Kubernetes herding the containers.

The success metric was silence. No reader ever noticed a thing: the paper published every single day while we swapped the engine mid-flight.
moral: Moral: the best migrations are the boring ones.
postcardTo: everyone who reads the news without thinking about queues

facts.heading: constraint
facts.fact: the paper came out every day, mid-migration

facts.heading: result
facts.fact: the old stack went dark in 2025

caseStudy:
## The starting point

The Post-Gazette's publishing stack had been accreting since roughly 2016. One enormous application that did everything and feared nothing, least of all my weekends. Deploys were [? how deploys worked, and how scary ?], and every change carried the whole system's risk.

The constraints were plain. One engineer. Bare metal on premises, no cloud autoscaling to hide behind. And a paper that publishes every single day, so there was no maintenance window big enough to swap the stack in one move.

> Drew the boxes before the arrows. Always the boxes first.

## The shape of it

Services stay small, own their data, and talk through RabbitMQ instead of calling each other directly. Varnish absorbs read traffic at the edge, so a couple million visits a month never becomes a couple million origin hits. MetalLB hands out real IPs, because bare metal does not come with a load balancer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  lb[metallb] --> ing[nginx ingress]
  ing --> va[varnish cache]
  va --> bus{{rabbitmq · the event bus}}
  bus --> svc[python services · x29]
  svc --> mdb[(mongodb)]
  svc --> rds[(redis)]
```

The rule that made 29 services survivable for one keeper: every service looks the same. Same repo layout, same base image, same health checks, same way onto and off the bus. The novelty budget goes to the problem, not the plumbing.

## Migrating while the paper ships

The monolith came apart one capability at a time. Peel a function off, stand it up as a service on the bus, route traffic to it, watch it for a while, then delete the old code path. Repeat 29 times. The first peel was [? the first service extracted, and why it went first ?].

Order mattered more than speed. Low-risk, high-annoyance pieces went first, to prove the pattern. The scary ones went last, once the platform had earned trust.

:::facts
timeline: [? months to production, months refining ?]
first peel: [? service name ?]
scariest peel: [? service name, and why it waited ?]
:::

## What went wrong

> The lamp swap happened at night. Obviously.

Events everywhere is worth it for the decoupling, and you pay for it in debugging: tracing a story across queues is harder than reading a stack trace. [? how you trace across the bus today ?]

![the first screenshot, from the station archive](first-screenshot.svg)

The mistake worth admitting: [? the thing that actually broke once, and what it taught you ?]

## What I'd change now

- Fewer, fatter services from day one. The honest number is probably [? your honest number ?].
- [? the second thing you'd do differently ?]
- [? the opinion only someone who ran this would have ?]

## Outcomes

:::outcomes
29 services | in production, uniform, one keeper
~2M visits/mo | served through the platform
0 missed papers | it published every day, the whole way through
old stack: dark | decommissioned 2025 · see light no. 014
:::

title: Newsroom plumbing
shortDesc: A publishing workflow a real paper runs on, every day, deadlines and all.
body:
Reporters write, editors edit, and somewhere between 'done' and 'published' lives an entire hidden city of pipes. I designed and built a good chunk of that city: APIs, storage, caching, the works.

It runs on deadlines that do not move for anybody. The plumbing holds. Mostly nobody knows it exists, which is exactly how plumbing should work.
moral: Moral: if they never think about you, you did it right.

facts.heading: role
facts.fact: APIs, storage, caching · design and build

facts.heading: constraint
facts.fact: deadlines that do not move for anybody

facts.heading: result
facts.fact: nobody knows it exists. that is the review.

title: 100k good mornings
shortDesc: An automated newsletter that beats the sunrise into six figures of inboxes.
body:
Every morning, before coffee exists, an automated system assembles the day’s news and delivers it to over 100,000 inboxes. It has done this more reliably than I get out of bed.

The fun part was making it boring: retries, queues, monitoring, and a healthy paranoia about what happens at 4am when nobody is watching.
moral: Moral: automation is just kindness to your future self, scheduled.
postcardTo: 100,000 people who are not awake yet

facts.heading: design goal
facts.fact: boring on purpose: retries, queues, monitoring

title: Meo Wave Race
shortDesc: A cat/boat racing game for the CS50 final. The cat is the boat. Ask me anything.
body:
For the CS50 final I made a racing game where a cat is also a boat. I modeled the cat. I modeled the waves. I wrote shaders. I regret nothing.

It shipped, it runs, and it remains my entire game development career: one game made, one game finished, undefeated.
postcardTo: anyone who says scope creep is inevitable
postcardFrom: a cat that is also a boat

title: This website
shortDesc: The site you're on. Rebuilt whenever the mood strikes, which is often.
body:
You are here. Previous versions had a space theme and a particle system that heated homes across Pittsburgh. This one has a lighthouse, postcards, and a hobby graveyard.

It gets rebuilt whenever the mood strikes, which historically is often. Consider this postcard a snapshot of a moving target.
moral: Moral: the portfolio is also the hobby.
postcardTo: future justin, who will redesign this

facts.heading: uptime
facts.fact: the light runs on a schedule. ish.

facts.heading: risk
facts.fact: total rebuild whenever the mood strikes

title: The home lab
shortDesc: Plex, HTPC, and a small fleet of machines that mostly behave.
body:
A Plex server, an HTPC, CachyOS on the desktop, and a small fleet of machines that mostly behave. This is the one hobby that survives every purge, because it is just the day job wearing pajamas.

Current status, as always: one tweak from perfect. The tweak changes weekly. Perfect remains at a fixed distance, like the horizon.
postcardTo: the family, who just wanted to watch a movie

facts.heading: audience
facts.fact: the family, mostly for the movies

title: The old publishing stack
shortDesc: A lot, that did very little. Put out by light no. 002, one piece at a time.
body:
The monolith itself deserves an honest entry: it ran a real newspaper for the better part of a decade, and it never asked permission to page me. Every system on this coast exists because this one existed first.

It was decommissioned in 2025, capability by capability, until one night there was nothing left to turn off. No wake. The queue drained, the DNS flipped, and the coast got quieter.
moral: Moral: a good platform is measured by what it lets you turn off.
postcardFrom: a system that finally got to rest

facts.heading: scope
facts.fact: a lot, that did very little

facts.heading: fate
facts.fact: decommissioned by no. 002, piece by piece

title: The Quick Reply Bot
shortDesc: A chatbot that answers faster than I can, which was the whole problem.
body:
I wrote a bot to answer the same three questions in a group chat so I would not have to. It got faster than me, then funnier than me, then more popular than me. I keep it running out of respect.

Every so often it says something I wish I had thought of first. That is the tinkering tax.
moral: Moral: automate the boring parts, brace for the rest.
postcardTo: everyone who has ever asked the same question twice
postcardFrom: a bot with better comebacks than me

facts.heading: made for
facts.fact: a group chat tired of repeating itself

facts.heading: personality
facts.fact: funnier than intended, faster than me

title: Tap Code
shortDesc: A desk lamp that blinks my initial in morse when a build finishes.
body:
A weekend build: an Arduino, a relay, and a desk lamp that taps out a single letter in morse whenever CI goes green. Entirely unnecessary. I have not turned it off once.

Coworkers assumed it was a bug for the first month. It is, technically, the opposite of a bug.
moral: Moral: the best hobbies solve problems nobody has.
postcardTo: the CI pipeline, who has no idea it has a fan

facts.heading: made for
facts.fact: a weekend and an idle relay

facts.heading: uptime
facts.fact: has not been turned off once
